message.group-title: Intensive Care and Critical Care Medicine
message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    During the COVID-19 pandemic, the need for noninvasive respiratory support devices has dramatically increased, sometimes exceeding hospital capacity. The full-face Decathlon snorkeling mask, EasyBreath
                    <jats:sup>®</jats:sup>
                    (EB
                    <jats:sup>®</jats:sup>
                    mask), has been adapted to deliver continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) as an emergency respiratory interface. We aimed to assess the performance of this modified EB
                    <jats:sup>®</jats:sup>
                    mask.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    CPAP set at 5, 10, and 15 cmH
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    O was delivered to 10 healthy volunteers with a high-flow system generator set at 40, 80, and 120 L min
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    and with a turbine-driven ventilator during both spontaneous and loaded (resistor) breathing. Inspiratory CO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    partial pressure (PiCO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    ), pressure inside the mask, breathing pattern and electrical activity of the diaphragm (EAdi) were measured at all combinations of CPAP/flows delivered, with and without the resistor.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Using the high-flow generator set at 40 L min
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    , the PiCO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    significantly increased and the system was unable to maintain the target CPAP of 10 and 15 cmH
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    O and a stable pressure within the respiratory cycle; conversely, the turbine-driven ventilator did. EAdi significantly increased with flow rates of 40 and 80 L min
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    but not at 120 L min
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    and with the turbine-driven ventilator.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    EB
                    <jats:sup>®</jats:sup>
                    mask can be safely used to deliver CPAP only under strict constraints, using either a high-flow generator at a flow rate greater than 80 L min
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    , or a high-performance turbine-driven ventilator.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title:
Performance of EasyBreath
                  <sup>®</sup>
                  Decathlon Snorkeling mask for Delivering Continuous Positive Airway Pressure

reference.unstructured: Dashboard by the center for systems science and engineering (CSSE) at Johns Hopkins University., https://gisanddata.maps.arcgis.com/apps/opsdashboard/index.html#/bda7594740fd40299423467b48e9ecf6 (2020).

Kroo, L. et al. Pneumask: Modified Full-Face Snorkel Masks as Reusable Personal Protective Equipment for Hospital Personnel. medRxiv 2020.04.24.20078907, doi: https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.04.24.20078907 (2020).
